assets.name: lecture_10_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: Candidate 01 is the most useful note-writing frame because it preserves both parts of the moment: the central-spin neighbor sketch on the left and the most complete visible form of the bracketed mean-field expression and sigma-bar self-consistency equation on the right. Candidate 02 crops more of the right-hand equation, and candidates 03 and 04 lose the equation almost entirely.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: that's our equation that determines sigma bar.

assets.name: lecture_10_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: Candidate 04 is the clearest and most stable frame of this moment. The rewritten scalar equation is fully legible, the lecturer’s hand is no longer touching or blurring the board, and the first hint of the graph at the far right is still visible. Candidates 01 and 02 are similar but less settled, while candidate 03 is weakened by the moving hand near the equation.

assets.name: lecture_10_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: Candidate 03 is the best balance of clarity and completeness. It preserves the lattice sketch on the left and, more importantly, shows the new identification \(\sigma=-1\) clearly on the right. Earlier candidates catch the writing in progress, while candidate 04 has a slightly fuller grid but begins to obscure the key notation as the lecturer starts the next item.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: Sigma equals minus one means no particle on that site.
assets.caption_hint: Sigma equals minus one on the lattice

assets.name: lecture_10_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: Candidate 02 gives the best overall balance for note writing. It keeps the right-hand energy-counting list fully legible while still preserving enough of the left-hand interaction term to show the board context. Candidate 01 is more occluded by Susskind’s back, and candidates 03 and 04 show the left equation more clearly but crop away too much of the right-hand list, which is the main new content here.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: So let me suppose I plotted the energy
assets.caption_hint: Energy counting for one and two particles
